narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and a competitive LSAT score of 37, you brought strong credentials to your law school application, along with the advantage of coming from a high family income bracket. Your plan to attend full-time also aligns with the vast majority of students in the dataset, about 93% of whom pursue that path. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 49.7% probability that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of around 5.5; this suggests your later academic performance placed you in the bottom deciles compared to peers, signaling potential challenges in sustaining high achievement through law school and bar prep. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a meaningful positive influence by being right around the typical 3.21 average, showing you have the foundational academic strength to succeed. Similarly, your LSAT score edged slightly above the average of 36.7, providing another boost toward a pass prediction and highlighting your aptitude for the rigors of legal reasoning. Overall, while your test scores and prior GPA helped pull the odds in your favor, the dip in your later high school deciles carried the strongest weight, outweighing those strengths in the model's eyes when compared to students who typically pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a supportive background—many applicants with similar profiles overcome early hurdles—but the model saw your Year 3 performance as the biggest red flag relative to successful bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building a stronger senior-year record to improve your decile rankings, as admissions teams often value demonstrated upward trends. You might also seek targeted LSAT or bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself to further solidify your profile. Reapplying after addressing that key weakness could shift the prediction dramatically in your favor. Keep pushing; your baseline strengths position you well for another shot at law school.
